content.problemStatement:
Chef has devised a new algorithm which works as follows. It takes an array aaa with nnn integers (a0,a1,…,an−1a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_{n-1}a0​,a1​,…,an−1​), and returns another array bbb.

`pseudo_sort($a$):
- Array $b$ is initially empty.
- For i = 0; i $\leq n$-1; i++:
    - If $b$ is empty, append $a_i$ to $b$.
    - Otherwise check if $a_i \ge$ last element of $b$
        - If yes:
            - Append $a_i$ to $b$. 
        - If no:
            - Do nothing and continue
- Return $b$
`This algorithm will return the array bbb whose elements are sorted in non-decreasing order.

Your task is to delete at most one element from the array aaa such that after calling this function on the modified array aaa, the length of the returned array bbb is maximum possible. Return the maximum possible length of the returned array.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains an integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains an integer nnn.
The second line of each test case contains nnn space-separated integers denoting the array aaa.


Output Format
For each test case, output the answer in a new line.
constraints: Sum of n over all the test cases doesn't exceed 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains an integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains an integer n. The second line of each test case contains n space-separated integers denoting the array a.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the maximum possible length of the returned array b.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Testcase 1: Without deleting any element, when the algorithm is run on the original array, the returned array is b={1,2,3}b = \{1, 2, 3\}b={1,2,3}. This is the longest array possible, and hence the answer is 3.
Testcase 2: After deleting the second element, the array aaa becomes {1,2,2,4}\{1, 2, 2, 4\}{1,2,2,4}. When the algorithm is run on this array, the returned array is b={1,2,2,4}b = \{1, 2, 2, 4\}b={1,2,2,4}. This is the longest array possible, and hence the answer is 4.
Testcase 3: After deleting the third element, the array aaa becomes {1,3,2,3}\{1, 3, 2, 3\}{1,3,2,3}. When the algorithm is run on this array, the returned array is b={1,3,3}b = \{1, 3, 3\}b={1,3,3}. This is the longest array possible, and hence the answer is 3.

solution.approach: The approach is to iterate over the array and check if the current element is greater than or equal to the last element of the result array. If it is, append it to the result array. If not, check if removing the current element or the last element of the result array would result in a longer array. Keep track of the maximum length of the result array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n) — where T is the number of test cases and n is the maximum length of the array a
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) — for storing the result array

code.Python3:
# COMPLETE, RUNNABLE Python3 code
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline
T = int(input())
for _ in range(T):
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    max_len = 0
    for i in range(n):
        len_ = 0
        b = [a[i]]
        for j in range(i + 1, n):
            if a[j] >= b[-1]:
                b.append(a[j])
                len_ += 1
        if len_ + 1 > max_len:
            max_len = len_ + 1
    print(max_len)
